preamble.summary: The community remembers a past restoration so stunning it felt like a dream — mouths filled with laughter, tongues with shouts of joy, even the surrounding nations acknowledging that the LORD had done great things. Then the psalm pivots to petition: restore us again, like desert streams suddenly filled with water. It closes with a promise that weeping will give way to harvest — those who go out crying, carrying seed, will return with joyful shouts, carrying sheaves.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm lives in the tension between memory and hope. The first three verses celebrate a past deliverance so overwhelming that the people could scarcely believe it was real — hayinu ke-cholmim ('we were like dreamers'). But the psalm does not stay in the past. Verse 4 shifts abruptly to petition: shuvah YHWH et shevitenu ('restore our fortunes, LORD'). The past rescue is not enough; the present still needs God's intervention. The agricultural metaphor in verses 5-6 bridges the gap: sowing in tears guarantees a harvest of joy. The metaphor is not sentimental — it is agricultural realism. In the ancient Near East, planting seed meant giving up precious grain that could feed your family, trusting that the ground would return it multiplied. The tears are real loss; the harvest is real gain.
preamble.friction: The phrase be-shuv YHWH et shivat Tsiyon ('when the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion') has been traditionally linked to the return from Babylonian exile, but the Hebrew shivat could also be read as shevut ('captivity') — 'when the LORD returned the captives of Zion.' The psalm may predate or postdate the exile; the language is deliberately open enough to apply to any major restoration. The dream-like quality of the remembered deliverance suggests distance — either temporal or emotional — from the event.
preamble.connections: The phrase hayinu ke-cholmim ('we were like dreamers') echoes the disorientation of Isaiah 29:7-8, where nations besieging Jerusalem vanish like a dream. The image of streams in the Negev (afikot ba-Negev) connects to Joel 3:18, where water flows from the house of the LORD. The sowing-and-reaping metaphor is developed extensively in Hosea 10:12 ('Sow righteousness, reap faithful love') and Galatians 6:7-9.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֗יר הַֽמַּ֫עֲל֥וֹת בְּשׁ֣וּב יְ֭הוָה אֶת־שִׁיבַ֣ת צִיּ֑וֹן הָ֝יִ֗ינוּ כְּחֹלְמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: When the LORD turned again the captivity of Zion, we were like them that dream.
verses.rendering:
A song of ascents.
When the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion,
we were like dreamers.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase shivat Tsiyon ('the fortunes/captivity of Zion') uses a construction that can mean either 'restored fortunes' or 'returned captives.' The ambiguity may be intentional — restoration is both economic recovery and homecoming. The comparison ke-cholmim ('like dreamers') captures the disbelief that accompanies sudden, overwhelming deliverance. The people could not process what was happening in real time; it seemed unreal, too good to be waking life. This is not mystical dreaming but the cognitive shock of unexpected rescue.
translator_notes: The ketiv-qere variation between shivat and shevut has generated extensive scholarly discussion. shivat (from shuv, 'to return') suggests restored fortunes; shevut (from shavah, 'to take captive') suggests returned captives. Both readings are theologically coherent. We render 'restored the fortunes' as the broader term that encompasses both possibilities.

key_terms.semantic_range: dreamers, those who dream, those in a dream-state
key_terms.note: From chalam ('to dream'). In the Hebrew Bible, dreams are often vehicles of divine revelation (Genesis 28, Daniel 2), but here the sense is different — the people are not receiving a vision but experiencing reality that feels like a vision. The restoration is so improbable that consciousness cannot fully accept it.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֤ז יִמָּלֵ֪א שְׂח֡וֹק פִּינוּ֮ וּלְשׁוֹנֵ֢נוּ רִ֫נָּ֥ה אָ֣ז יֹאמְר֣וּ בַגּוֹיִ֑ם הִגְדִּ֥יל יְ֝הוָ֗ה לַעֲשׂ֥וֹת עִם־אֵֽלֶּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Then was our mouth filled with laughter, and our tongue with singing: then said they among the heathen, The LORD hath done great things for them.
verses.rendering:
Then our mouths were filled with laughter
and our tongues with shouts of joy.
Then they said among the nations,
"The LORD has done great things for these people."
translator_notes: The double az ('then') creates a two-beat rhythm: then laughter, then recognition. The verb yimmale ('was filled') describes mouths overflowing — not polite smiles but uncontainable laughter. The word rinnah ('joyful shouting, ringing cry') is loud, public, and physical. The nations' testimony — higdil YHWH la'asot im elleh ('the LORD has done great things with these') — is remarkable: even outsiders can see that this deliverance is God's work.

verses.text_hebrew: הִגְדִּ֣יל יְ֭הוָה לַעֲשׂ֥וֹת עִמָּ֗נוּ הָיִ֥ינוּ שְׂמֵחִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD hath done great things for us; whereof we are glad.
verses.rendering:
The LORD has done great things for us —
we are filled with joy.
translator_notes: Israel takes the nations' words and makes them its own confession: higdil YHWH la'asot immanu ('the LORD has done great things with us'). The preposition im ('with') rather than le ('for') subtly implies partnership — God did great things with us, not merely to us. The response hayinu semechim ('we are/were joyful') uses the same root as Psalm 122:1 (samachti), linking pilgrimage joy to the joy of national restoration.

verses.text_kjv: Turn again our captivity, O LORD, as the streams in the south.
verses.rendering:
Restore our fortunes, LORD,
like streams in the Negev.
verses.expanded_rendering: The imperative shuvah ('restore, return, turn back') marks the psalm's pivot from memory to petition. The past deliverance is celebrated, but the present is not yet fully restored. The simile ka-afiqim ba-Negev ('like streams/channels in the Negev') draws on a dramatic natural phenomenon: the Negev desert has dry streambeds (wadis) that are barren and cracked for months, then suddenly fill with rushing water after rain. The transformation is total and sudden — from dust to torrent. The psalm asks God to do the same: flood our dry situation with sudden, overwhelming restoration.
translator_notes: The afiqim ('channels, streambeds') of the Negev are visible features of the landscape a pilgrim would know. The image is not gentle rain but flash restoration — water appearing where there was none, life erupting from what appeared dead.

verses.text_kjv: They that sow in tears shall reap in joy.
verses.rendering:
Those who sow in tears
will reap with shouts of joy.
translator_notes: The contrast between bedim'ah ('in tears') and berinnah ('with joyful shouting') is absolute. The agricultural metaphor is not abstract: sowing required surrendering seed grain that could have fed the family. In a famine or hardship, planting was an act of faith — you gave up what you had in hope of a future harvest. The tears are the cost of the investment; the joy is the harvest's return.

verses.text_hebrew: הָ֘ל֤וֹךְ יֵלֵ֨ךְ ׀ וּבָכֹה֮ נֹשֵׂ֢א מֶֽשֶׁךְ־הַ֫זָּ֥רַע בֹּֽא־יָב֥וֹא בְרִנָּ֑ה נֹ֝שֵׂ֗א אֲלֻמֹּתָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: He that goeth forth and weepeth, bearing precious seed, shall doubtless come again with rejoicing, bringing his sheaves with him.
verses.rendering:
The one who goes out weeping,
carrying the bag of seed,
will surely come back with shouts of joy,
carrying his sheaves.
verses.expanded_rendering: The infinitive absolute construction halokh yelekh ('he will surely go') and bo yavo ('he will surely come') create emphasis through repetition — the going is certain and the returning is certain. The weeping farmer carries meshekh hazara ('the trail/pouch of seed') — his last reserves, scattered into dirt with no guarantee. But the psalm guarantees the return: noseh alumotav ('carrying his sheaves'). The alumot ('sheaves') are bundles of harvested grain — the visible, tangible proof that the investment of tears produced abundance. The pilgrim singing this psalm on the road to Jerusalem is both the weeping sower and the returning harvester, depending on where they are in the journey of faith.
translator_notes: The phrase meshekh hazara is unusual. meshekh may mean 'drawing, trailing' (from mashakh) or 'bag, pouch.' We render 'bag of seed' for clarity, understanding it as the container from which the sower draws seed to scatter. The image is of a farmer walking through a field, hand reaching into a bag, scattering grain through tears.
